---
title: "Mapping Crotaphytus collaris Sightings in Oklahoma"
format: html
execute: 
    echo: true
    warning: false
---

## Question

Where are iNaturalist sightings of *Crotaphytus collaris* concentrated in Oklahoma, and what patterns or biases appear in the data?

## Why this project matters

Species occurrence data are often used to infer distribution, but observational datasets can reflect both biology and human sampling behavior. This project explores both.

## Data source

iNaturalist observations API. The dataset was pulled programmatically from the observations endpoint and flattened into a CSV for analysis. Link to code here. 

## Approach

- Filter observations to Oklahoma
- Clean location data
- Plot points on a map
- Examine clustering and possible sources of bias

# Load packages

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
```

# Load data

```{python}
df = pd.read_csv("data/processed/inat_collared_lizard_oklahoma.csv")
df.head()
```

# Basic cleaning

```{python}
df = df.dropna(subset=["latitude", "longitude"]).copy()
df["observed_on"] = pd.to_datetime(df["observed_on"], errors="coerce")
df["year"] = df["observed_on"].dt.year

#remove captive observations if present
if "captive" in df.columns:
    df = df[df["captive"] != True].copy()
```

## Map Styling function
```{python}
def style_oklahoma_map(fig, title):
    fig.update_traces(
        marker=dict(
            size=7,
            opacity=0.75,
            line=dict(width=0.4, color="black")
        )
    )

    fig.update_layout(
        title=dict(
            text=title,
            x=0.5,
            xanchor="center",
            y=0.95
        ),
        paper_bgcolor="white",
        plot_bgcolor="white",
        margin=dict(l=10, r=10, t=40, b=10),
        legend=dict(
            title="Quality grade",
            x=0.0,
            y=0.5,
            xanchor="left",
            yanchor="middle",
            bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.6)",
            bordercolor="black",
            borderwidth=0.5
        )
    )    
    
    fig.update_geos(
        scope="usa",
        center=dict(lat=35.5, lon=-97.5),
        projection_scale=6,
        bgcolor="white",
        showland=True,
        landcolor="white",
        showcountries=True,
        countrycolor="black",
        countrywidth=0.8,
        showsubunits=True,
        subunitcolor="black",
        subunitwidth=1.2,
        showlakes=False,
        showcoastlines=False
    )

    return fig
```

## Basic sighting map

```{python}
fig1 = px.scatter_geo(
    df,
    lat="latitude",
    lon="longitude",
    hover_name="common_name",
    hover_data={"observed_on": True, "quality_grade": True},
    opacity=0.7
)

fig1 = style_oklahoma_map(fig1, "Collared Lizard Observations in Oklahoma")

fig1
```

## Observation quality counts

```{python}
fig2 = px.scatter_geo(
    df,
    lat="latitude",
    lon="longitude",
    color="quality_grade",
    hover_name="common_name",
    hover_data={"observed_on": True},
    opacity=0.75,
    color_discrete_map={
        "research": "#3B82F6",
        "casual": "#EF4444",
        "needs_id": "#F59E0B"
    }
)

# bold text helper function
def make_bold(name):
    return f"<b>{name}</b>"

#add relevant refuges
refuges = pd.DataFrame({
    "name": [
        "Wichita Mountains Wildlife Refuge",
        "Osage Reservation"
    ],
    "latitude": [34.73, 36.75],   # adjust if needed
    "longitude": [-98.62, -96.30]
})

fig2.add_scattergeo(
    lat=refuges["latitude"],
    lon=refuges["longitude"],
    mode="markers",
    marker=dict(
        size=15,
        symbol="diamond",
        color="black",
        line=dict(width=1, color="white")
    ),
    name="Reference sites"
)

fig2 = style_oklahoma_map(fig2, "Collared Lizard Observations by iNaturalist Quality Grade")

fig2.add_annotation(
    x=0.75,
    y=0.69,
    xref="paper",
    yref="paper",
    text="<b>Osage Reservation</b>",
    showarrow=False,
    font=dict(size=12, color="black"),
    bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.7)",
    bordercolor="rgba(0,0,0,0)",
    borderpad=3
)

fig2.add_annotation(
    x=0.53,
    y=0.34,
    xref="paper",
    yref="paper",
    text="<b>Wichita Mountains Wildlife Refuge</b>",
    showarrow=False,
    font=dict(size=12, color="black"),
    bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.7)",
    bordercolor="rgba(0,0,0,0)",
    borderpad=3
)

fig2
```



## Observation Patterns Over Time 
```{python}
counts = df.groupby(df["observed_on"].dt.year).size().reset_index(name="count")

fig = px.line(
    counts,
    x="observed_on",
    y="count",
    title="Number of Observations Over Time"
)

fig
```

## Key takeaways

- Observations cluster in accessible or highly visited locations (Wichita Mountains Wildlife Refuge & The Osage Reservation)
- Reported occurrence does not necessarily equal true distribution
- Visualization helps reveal likely sampling bias quickly

## Tools used

- iNaturalist API
- Python
- Quarto
- Plotly

## Next steps

- Compare iNaturalist and GBIF records
- Explore habitat or land-use overlays
